items.title: Are institutions about to rotate back into stocks? US pension funds are estimated to purchase +$13.8 billion of US equities by the quarter-end, accord...
items.description_html:
Are institutions about to rotate back into stocks?

US pension funds are estimated to purchase +$13.8 billion of US equities by the quarter-end, according to Goldman Sachs.

This is larger than 97% of all monthly purchases over the last 3 years and 93% since January 2000.

By comparison, pension funds have sold an average of -$1.8 billion per month since 2000, while the all-time high purchase was +$80.6 billion during the 2020 pandemic.

This comes as US pension funds are set to rebalance from bonds to equities to maintain target allocations after the recent decline in stocks.

As a consequence, an equivalent amount of bonds will be sold to fund the purchases.

Equities are set for large inflows.

items.title: RT John Kemp: Political economy of the energy crisis In a food or fuel crisis, it is often sauve qui peut — every man (or country) for himself. Closu...
items.description_html:
RT John Kemp
Political economy of the energy crisis

In a food or fuel crisis, it is often sauve qui peut — every man (or country) for himself.

Closure of the Strait of Hormuz is creating a worldwide shortage of oil and gas, triggering a scramble to protect fuel supplies by households, businesses and governments, especially in Asia.

But the metastasising web of price controls, subsidies, export restrictions and hoarding, while understandable, threaten to make the shortages worse especially for the world’s poorest people.

Most government interventions attempt to mitigate the impact of energy shortages on low income households and essential businesses by shifting some of the burden onto other groups.

Export bans, price controls, rationing, subsidies, taxation, borrowing and debt all attempt to shift the distribution of costs and benefits from the one that would prevail under pure market forces.

But while the distribution may change the total amount of demand restraint or destruction to meet reduced supply remains the same.

In practice, government interventions are most likely to shift the burden from higher-income countries that can afford to subsidise fuel use to poorer countries that cannot.

Governments in North America, Europe, Japan and even China may be able to shield their citizens from the full impact of rising prices — but at the expense of more demand destruction in poor countries in South and Southeast Asia ...

items.title: Closing FY2026 - 9 Months ago I took the leap of faith and went back into startup mode and Here’s how it’s going - Got myself SEBI Registered in 202...
items.description_html:
Closing FY2026 - 9 Months ago I took the leap of faith and went back into startup mode and Here’s how it’s going

- Got myself SEBI Registered in 2025
- Grew Socials from an average of 320k subs to almost 450k in 9months on Insta and YT
- Distribution across platforms grew to 1Mn+
- Started with a team of 6, Today we are at 18 (3 remote)
- Launched Smallcases on October 2nd 2025, Beat our yearly target of 1,000 subs in 1 month, revised it to 3,000 subs for October 2026, we are 80% there.
- Crossed 26 Cr in AUM, aiming to cross 50 Cr soon
- Launched 7BAR - Swing Trading Cohort, Crossed 3,300 Members in 9 months, our fastest growing vertical
- Both 7BAR and Smallcase operate at 80% Margins today, lean team, Good Value add
- Did 3 Offline events in Bangalore, Mumbai and Jaipur. Next is Hyderabad and Chennai - We don't make money on this as its a part of our Investor education initiative

Big Bets and Cash Burners
- Building a Fintech app in Stealth, Will be launching in the next 3-4months - Bootstrapped
- Will be along the lines of Stock Advisory and My distribution to start with
- Aim to get 7,000 Paid Users in Year 1 without Ad Spend (low estimates)
- Invested in 3 Shadow Channels on Social Media to support the App
- Team size should go up to 25 and our current office would sadly max out.

Investments
- Last year I Invested/ Incubated a Studio Company called Studio Ionique, goal was to grow my channel, service other customers and build many IP channels to help our products
- As of year closing (9months), The Biz closed at 1.06cr topline
- We have built 9 IP channels out of which 3 have crossed 50k subs (without my face) rest in incubation phase
- Social Media Services wing - We are currently working with 5 customers only, generating an average of 17L a month (3.5L average)
- Client breakup - 1 AI, 1 SAAS, 1 Global, 1 Youtube Biz, 1 Market Participant
- Year 2 should, at minimum close at 2.7cr topline for Studio

What's next
- We will be applying for PMS/AIF in 2027
- Converting from Individual RA to Corporate RA
- Due to having only an RA license, We are rejecting many customers who want us to manage their money/PF today
- Will have 1 more big launch this year by December 2026 that should generate 1cr+ revenue in 365days of launch with 50%+ margins
- Got in 2 co-founders, 1 who manages the entire Research side and the other who will build the tech stack

What I Need to Improve?
- Need to focus more on My Socials, kinda let it fall while building the biz from scratch. Realized the importance of having a co-founder haha
- Earlier we were a lean team, now we are scaling a bit too fast, hence have to start improving the hiring process.
- Earlier, we hired everyone on the metric of revenue realization - Now I need to hire people who won’t directly contribute to increasing revenue but more towards branding and comms
- Started focusing on health, Got inspired by my peers mainly @VaibhavSisinty , bro got ripped while juggling 100 things and he’s just 100ft away from my office. So no excuses for me now
- Mental Health kept going for a toss - Started up at the same time I became a dad. So There is a constant Guilt that I’m not spending a lot of time with my son during this stage, will fix that

Overall Both Businesses combined should be hitting 5cr+ topline next year while being very profitable, Full Steam Ahead🙌 Goal is to build a very good wealth management firm. Baby steps

items.title: RT Vasant Shetty | Building Mundhe Banni: I have known @deepakshenoy on Twitter for a long time, and I’ve always enjoyed reading his views and though...
items.description_html:
RT Vasant Shetty | Building Mundhe Banni
I have known @deepakshenoy on Twitter for a long time, and I’ve always enjoyed reading his views and thoughts on finance, investing, savings, the stock market, and all of that. He’s a great mind that I truly admire.

I had heard him speak in Kannada on a podcast called Taleharate a few years ago, run by a dear friend, @zeusisdead, and I knew that he could comfortably converse in Kannada. When we reached out with a request to come on @mundhebanni, he immediately said yes, showed up on time, and we had a fascinating discussion.

I also learned a great deal about what it really takes when someone is deeply passionate about something and wants to build a career around it. Deepak is an NITK Surathkal graduate and spent at least the first 10 years of his career building in tech, before pivoting into something he was truly passionate about, finance. He started out with a blog, and that blog’s success eventually led to the creation of one of India’s most respected investment research and portfolio management companies, Capitalmind, managing thousands of crores.

In a signature Mundhe Banni style, this story is a mix of three things: his personal journey, professional success, and his advice and perspective on personal finance.

I also learned that he was in Mumbai on the day of 26/11 and had narrowly escaped the tragedy!

The full episode is dropping this evening.

Watch the trailer 👇

Subscribe to @mundhebanni YT to get notified:
https://www.youtube.com/@mundhebanni

@akaranth @Shishir_S_U @kodlady

items.title: RT Tushar Pandey: You might see the government is suddenly obsessed with getting a gas pipe into your kitchen (PNG) instead of just delivering a cylin...
items.description_html:
RT Tushar Pandey
You might see the government is suddenly obsessed with getting a gas pipe into your kitchen (PNG) instead of just delivering a cylinder (LPG). It actually comes down to a massive math problem we are trying to solve.

Most people think gas is just gas but they’re actually totally different chemicals. LPG is the stuff in the red cylinders (propane & butane) and is basically a scrap product. When we refine crude oil to make petrol and diesel, we only get a tiny sliver of LPG (about 3%).

Even though India has world-class refineries, we just can’t squeeze enough LPG out of a barrel of oil to feed 33 crore households. This forces us to import a staggering 60% of our LPG, mostly from the Middle East. If a war breaks out or a shipping lane like the Strait of Hormuz gets blocked, those cylinders stop showing up at your door as usual.

Natural Gas (which is then sent as PNG), on the other hand, is a primary fuel (mostly methane). We actually have plenty of it sitting in our own ground in India. While we still import some, we aren't nearly as dependent on the Middle East for it as we are for LPG.

By moving city folks onto pipelines, the government is essentially freeing up the limited supply of cylinders for rural villages where you can't easily dig pipes. It’s a huge logistical reshuffle to make sure a crisis in West Asia doesn't leave the masses unable to cook.

There's also another angle here. Countries like the US and Qatar don't even need refineries to get LPG, they just suck it straight out of massive "wet" gas wells. India doesn't have those specific types of wells. So, for us to keep using LPG, we have to keep buying expensive crude oil and hoping the 3% we get as a by-product is enough.

Over the long term, in hindsight, it was a losing game. Indian bureaucracy should have envisaged this and prepared in advance, esp. when Ujjwala Scheme more than doubled the number of LPG connections to ~32cr. That was a strategic mistake.

Switching to PNG is basically India’s way of saying that let's stop relying on a byproduct that we lack domestically and start using the methane (natural gas) we actually produce at home.

It’s safer, it’s underground (so it's harder to disrupt during a war), and it keeps the supply chain running even when global oil prices go crazy. For an average person, it’s the difference between waiting for a truck that might not come and having a tap that never runs dry.

items.title: RT Tarique Rahman: March 25, 1971 is observed as Genocide Day. On the occasion of Genocide Day, 1 pay my deepest respect to all the martyrs. In the hi...
items.description_html:
RT Tarique Rahman
March 25, 1971 is observed as Genocide Day. On the occasion of Genocide Day, 1 pay my deepest respect to all the martyrs.

In the history of freedom-loving Bangladesh, 25 March 1971 remains one of the most disgraceful and brutal days. On that dark night, the Pakistani occupation forces carried out one of the most heinous genocides in history against the unarmed people of Bangladesh in the name of 'Operation Searchlight".

They indiscriminately opened fire on teachers, intellectuals and innocent civilians at various places, including Dhaka University, Pilkhana and Rajarbāgh Police Lines, killing many people.

The genocide of 25 March was a pre-planned massacre. Why this organised killing spree could not be resisted remains a matter of historical research regarding the visible role of the political leadership of that time.

However, on the night of 25 March, the 8th East Bengal Regiment in Chattogram formally initiated armed resistance against the genocide by declaring 'We Revolt'. Through this resistance to genocide, the long nine-month armed Liberation War began.

To convey the value and significance of independence to the present and future generations, it is essential to know about the genocide of 25 March as well. Let us all strive to honour the sacrifices of the martyrs by establishing in the state and society the spirit of the great Liberation War - equality, human dignity and social justice.

Let us work together to build a just, developed, prosperous, self-reliant and democratic Bangladesh.

I pray to the Almighty Allah to grant forgiveness and eternal peace to the departed souls of all the martyrs.

On the occasion of Genocide Day on 25 March. I wish every success to all the programmes organised to observe the day.

items.title: P/E is sometimes a flawed metric if used in isolation. It is also one of the most abused valuation ratios in markets. First, one should always look at...
items.description_html:
P/E is sometimes a flawed metric if used in isolation.

It is also one of the most abused valuation ratios in markets.

First, one should always look at normalised P/E. Reported earnings can get depressed due to exceptional losses, higher depreciation, one time provisions etc. In such cases, headline P/E does not reflect the true earning power of the business.

Second, P/E is not the right valuation metric for many sectors.

Capital intensive businesses, cyclicals, financials etc not to be valued on P/E basis.

items.description_html:
RT Aditya Raj Kaul
Direct Hit in Tel Aviv as Iran Fires New Missile Barrage, Multiple Impact Sites Reported in Israel.

Israel’s Magen David Adom reported that at least six people were wounded across four separate impact sites in the city. Dramatic footage from the scene shows heavy damage to a building, multiple vehicles destroyed, and thick smoke rising over parts of Tel Aviv.

Authorities believe it could be a new variant of a medium range ballistic missile or a cluster style warhead, dispersing 3 to 4 large explosives weighing approximately 100 kilograms each across central Israel.

items.title: Does ethanol blending actually save fossil fuel consumption. India is usually compared to Brazil Sharing the math below and answer is - Not Really Key...
items.description_html:
Does ethanol blending actually save fossil fuel consumption. India is usually compared to Brazil

Sharing the math below and answer is - Not Really

Key Take Away - It makes way more sense in Brazil as their sugar cane is rain fed, while in India we use quite a bit of irrigation

India saves ~27%. Brazil saves ~87%. The difference is almost entirely irrigation — Indian sugarcane is pumped with majorly coal-fired electricity, Brazilian cane is rainfed. India's ethanol program mostly swaps imported crude for domestic coal and gas. Brazil's genuinely displaces fossil fuels.

items.title: RT Richard Turrin: Your ID on the Blockchain Can Fight Tinder Fakes The biggest question remains: who should issue the ID? The technology used can be ...
items.description_html:
RT Richard Turrin
Your ID on the Blockchain Can Fight Tinder Fakes

The biggest question remains: who should issue the ID? The technology used can be debated after determining who controls the ID.

Should it be a form of state-issued Digital Public Infrastructure, as with India's Aadhaar, or should it pass to Web3 companies who believe in identity kept out of reach of the state?

While the debate between the state and Web3 remains lively, what most agree on is that this isn't a job for Google or Apple as much as they would like to have it.

Note that in the US, IRS electronic filing depends on "ID .me" a big tech solution paving the way for Google and Apple to take over.

Perhaps the best solution yet is the EU's EUDI wallet, which, while state-backed, supports decentralized identity (DID) standards, allowing users to own and manage their identity data rather than relying on central authorities like Google or Facebook.

While Web3 wants to be free of the state, it's hard to see such a DID system interacting with state-sponsored tax and social benefits.

Kudos to @andymukherjee70

items.title: RT Manan S. Bhatt: "Ambushed" #UntoldStoryArmy During a military training course in Mhow, India, the 1962 war with China broke out. My course was canc...
items.description_html:
RT Manan S. Bhatt
"Ambushed"
#UntoldStoryArmy

During a military training course in Mhow, India, the 1962 war with China broke out. My course was cancelled, and everyone returned to their units. However, I received special orders to stay put and await further instructions. Two days later, my posting to Jammu and Kashmir arrived – with immediate reporting required.

Having driven my car to Mhow, I had no choice but to take it back to Punjab, leave it there, and then travel on to J&K. Pressed for time, I decided to drive straight from Mhow to Delhi, a risky decision. One particularly dangerous stretch was between Gwalior and Agra, notorious for bandits and lawlessness.

Traveling alone at night through this area was foolish, but I pressed on in my trusty Hindustan-14. Around midnight, approaching a narrow pass through low hills, I found the road blocked by a fallen tree. It was an ambush. Turning back wasn't an option.

As I stopped, armed men materialized, their faces hidden. They searched me for valuables, taking my watch and wallet before moving on to my car and luggage. However, things took an unexpected turn when someone announced a "Sardar" had arrived.

Every one pulled back a little and there appeared a sturdy young man with a large mustache. As he drew near, he suddenly sprung to attention and gave me a smart salute and said ‘Sahib Ji, tussee ethay ki kar rahey ho.’ (Sir what are you doing here!)

From his voice and his face, latter day mustache notwithstanding, I recognized him. He was my tank gunner and had been discharged after his seven-year tenure of engagement. Those days soldiers were discharged after seven years and given no pension.

I responded to his query and asked him,’ Nahar Singh toon aye ki kam pharya hai (Nahar Singh what is this work you have taken on.) He said, ‘Sahib Ji admi nu kuch na kuch taan karna chaheda hai, welay beth kaay vi ki karna hai.’ (Sir one must do, some or the other work and there is no point in sitting idle!)

I told him, ‘tenu aasi eh taan nahi sakhlaya’ (we did not train you for such activity.) “Sahib ji tohati ambush laney di sikhlai kam ahh rahi hai. Sadi ambush wich koe nuks hai!.” (Sir your training in laying an ambush is proving useful. Do you find any fault with our ambush.)

He responded with a loud chuckle. He addressed his gang and said. “Dekheya sadhi regiment de officer kiney deelair hun, ekelay he rat nu is sarak tay chal rahey hun.” (do you see how brave are the officers of our regiment. They travel alone on this road at night.)

I asked him if his wife knew what work he was doing. To this he answered “Sarian gallaan wife nu thorian dassidian hun.

Oh soch di hai kay main police da kam kar rahaan hain.” (You don’t disclose everything to your wife. She thinks I am working for the police). At this one of them interjected that, “Police whi ehhoo kam kardi hai.” (Police too does this sort of work).

This raised much laughter amongst them. Then he told his men not to just stand watching but to quickly make tea for the sahib. All my stuff was put back in the car, and my watch and wallet returned. After a cup of tea and much bonhomie and handshaking I took leave of Nahar Singh and his gang.

A story by Lt Gen Harwant Singh

items.title: RT Intrinsic Compounding: India has 7.8 crore microfinance borrowers. Mostly women. Mostly rural. No collateral. No credit history. No bank willing to...
items.description_html:
RT Intrinsic Compounding
India has 7.8 crore microfinance borrowers. Mostly women. Mostly rural. No collateral. No credit history. No bank willing to serve them.

Without MFIs, they borrow from moneylenders at 40–120% annualised interest.

Microfinance breaks that trap by lending small, unsecured, through the social architecture of Joint Liability Groups (JLGs). 5-10 women from the same community who collectively guarantee each other's loans. In normal times, repayment rates rival the best secured lenders in the world 98–99%.

This is a long read. But if you want to understand how to think about cyclical financial businesses and what a disciplined operator looks like this is worth your time.

Full piece here 👇
https://soic.substack.com/p/microfinance-is-healing-early-signs

items.title: RT NoLimit: 🚨 SOMETHING BIG JUST HAPPENED: BlackRock just blocked investors from pulling their own money out. The world’s largest asset manager is...
items.description_html:
RT NoLimit
🚨 SOMETHING BIG JUST HAPPENED:

BlackRock just blocked investors from pulling their own money out.

The world’s largest asset manager is telling people: no, you can’t have your cash back.

This has never happened before.

BlackRock’s $26 billion private credit fund got hit with $1.2 billion in withdrawal requests this quarter.

Investors wanted 9.3% of their money back.

BlackRock said no. Capped it at 5%. Paid out $620 million and locked the rest.

That means almost HALF the people who wanted out couldn’t get out.

And it’s not just BlackRock.

Blackstone’s similar fund saw a RECORD 7.9% in redemption requests.

They had to raise their withdrawal cap and inject $400 million of their own money just to cover the demand.

Blue Owl straight up stopped honoring redemptions. Replaced them with IOUs.

BLK dropped 5%. KKR, Carlyle, Apollo, Ares, Blue Owl, and TPG all fell 5-6% with it.

The entire private credit sector sold off in a single day.

These funds lend money in illiquid loans. Loans that can’t be sold quickly.

So when too many investors want out at the same time, the fund doesn’t have the cash to pay everyone.

BlackRock also just wrote a separate $25 million loan down to ZERO.

It was valued at full price three months ago. Gone overnight.

JPMorgan’s Bill Eigen said it best: “Bad news often happens all at once. The opacity and the leverage in the sector is concerning.”

This is a $1.8 TRILLION industry.

– Rising oil.
– War in the Middle East.
– AI disrupting the software companies that borrowed heavily from these funds.
– Rate cuts off the table.

When the biggest funds in the world start telling investors you can’t have your money back…

That’s a MAJOR warning.

Btw, I’ve been an investor for more than 20 years, and when I make a new move in the market, I’ll announce it here publicly.

A lot of people will wish they followed me sooner.

items.title: RT Neel Chhabra: True market dominance requires controlling the critical dependencies... the inputs, the processes, the distribution, and ideally the ...
items.description_html:
RT Neel Chhabra
True market dominance requires controlling the critical dependencies... the inputs, the processes, the distribution, and ideally the customer relationship.

Carnegie controlled raw materials and transportation. Standard Oil controlled refining and distribution. AT&T controlled the entire network. IBM locked in both hardware and software. Microsoft owned the OS. Amazon built the logistics infrastructure. Apple controls chips, OS, apps, and retail. Tesla manufactures batteries, software, and handles direct sales.

The losers in each era? They rented someone else's infrastructure. They trusted partners. They optimized for margins instead of control. They played nice with existing systems instead of building new ones.

The pattern is unmistakable. Across 150 years and myriad of industries, the winners aren’t those with incrementally better products. The winners are those who understand that true market power comes from controlling what others need to reach customers.

It’s not just about vertical integration for efficiency’s sake, it’s about vertical integration as a strategic weapon. When you own the stack, you set the rules. You control the costs. You dictate the pace of innovation. You capture disproportionate value.

This is the timeless physics of market power. The names change. The industries evolve. But the fundamental insight remains: if you want to build an enduring franchise, don’t just compete on product. Compete on structure. Own the stack. Control the chokepoints. Make your business essential to the functioning of others.

That’s how monopolies are born. That’s how fortunes are made. And that’s the pattern that will define the next wave of industrial dominance, whether it’s in semiconductors, space infrastructure, or the picks-and-shovels of the AI revolution.
